data-quality.name: Floating title row
data-quality.what: Row 0 of `soil samples.xlsx` contains **"Alpine Soil Survey - Spring Campaign"** — a survey title embedded inside the data file. This creates a floating header that breaks automated parsing.
data-quality.why: Scripts expect the first row to be column headers or data. The extra title row shifts all row indices, causing errors in analysis pipelines and breaking tools that auto-detect headers.
data-quality.fix:
Remove the title row from the spreadsheet. Store the survey description in a separate README or data dictionary.

```
# Before
Row 0: Alpine Soil Survey - Spring Campaign  ← doesn't belong here
Row 1: (embedded note)
Row 2: id, col1, col2 ...  ← actual header

# After
Row 0: id, col1, col2 ...  ← header is first row
```

data-quality.name: Embedded note in data
data-quality.what: Row 1, column F of `soil samples.xlsx` contains the note *"site 7 sensor broken in March"* — a free-text note embedded inside the spreadsheet grid.
data-quality.why: Notes embedded in cells are invisible to data-loading scripts and are silently dropped when the file is converted or shared. They also prevent the file from being parsed as a clean rectangular table.
data-quality.fix:
Remove the note from the cell. Record it in a dedicated **notes** column in the data, or better yet, in the data dictionary or a separate events log.

```
# Option A: notes column in the data
id | ... | notes
7  | ... | sensor broken March 2026

# Option B: separate events log
events.txt: 2026-03 site 7 — sensor malfunction
```

data-quality.name: Ambiguous column names
data-quality.what: Column headers **col1, col2, col3** are meaningless placeholders. **temp** has no units. Without knowing what these columns measure, the data cannot be interpreted.
data-quality.why: Ambiguous names force every future user to hunt for external context. Analysis errors become more likely, and even the original researcher will forget the meaning within months.
data-quality.fix:
Rename columns to meaningful names **with units** where applicable:

| Old name | New name |
|---|---|
| col1 | soil_moisture_pct |
| col2 | organic_carbon_g_per_kg |
| col3 | bulk_density_g_per_cm3 |
| temp | temperature_C |

Document all column names and units in a data dictionary.

data-quality.name: Missing value: 'NA' string
data-quality.what: Cell (row 5, col2) of `soil samples.xlsx` contains the string **"NA"** as a missing-value indicator — while other cells use **'n/a'**, **'-999'**, **'??'**, or blank.
data-quality.why: Using multiple different codes for missing values breaks statistical software. Most tools only recognise one code; mixed codes cause silent errors where some missing values are treated as valid data.
data-quality.fix:
Pick **one** consistent missing-value code and document it in the data dictionary. The R/Python convention is `NA` (or `NaN`).

Replace all other codes ('n/a', '-999', '??', blanks) with that single code throughout the file.

data-quality.name: Missing value: 'n/a' string
data-quality.what: Cell (row 6, pH column) of `soil samples.xlsx` contains **"n/a"** — a second, differently-cased spelling of the not-available indicator used elsewhere as **'NA'**.
data-quality.why: Case-sensitive languages (Python, R) treat 'NA' and 'n/a' as different values. Mixed spellings mean some missing values appear as valid strings, distorting summary statistics.
data-quality.fix:
Standardise all missing values to a single code. Replace every variant ('n/a', 'NA', '-999', '??', blank) with the chosen standard — for example, `NA`.

Document the convention:
```
Missing values: NA (all variables)
```

data-quality.name: Missing value: blank cell
data-quality.what: Row 9 of `soil samples.xlsx` has **blank cells** in col1, col2, and col3. These blanks are an implicit missing-value code — it is impossible to tell if they mean 'not collected', 'below detection limit', or a data-entry error.
data-quality.why: Blank cells are ambiguous. When a file is re-saved, blanks can shift. They also interact unpredictably with pivot tables and summary functions. An explicit code like `NA` is always unambiguous.
data-quality.fix: Replace all blank cells in data rows with the chosen explicit missing-value code (e.g. `NA`). **Never leave cells blank in data columns** — use an explicit code and document it.

data-quality.name: Missing value: -999 sentinel
data-quality.what: Row 9, pH and temp columns of `soil samples.xlsx` contain **-999** — a numeric sentinel used as a missing-value code. Sentinels that look like plausible numbers are especially dangerous.
data-quality.why: Statistical functions silently include -999 in averages, min/max, and correlations. A mean pH incorporating -999 is nonsensical, and the error is invisible. This pattern has caused published retractions.
data-quality.fix: Replace -999 (and any other numeric sentinels) with an explicit missing-value code such as `NA`. **Never encode missing data as an extreme number.** Document the convention in the data dictionary.

data-quality.name: Missing value: '??' string
data-quality.what: Row 11, pH column of `soil samples.xlsx` contains **"??"** — an informal placeholder that does not appear in any documentation.
data-quality.why: Informal codes like '??' are easy to forget and hard to search for programmatically. They get confused with valid data and cause parse errors in strictly-typed formats (e.g. Parquet, databases).
data-quality.fix: Replace '??' with the standardised missing-value code (e.g. `NA`). Document the decision in the data dictionary so future users know there is exactly one missing-value code.
